project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve the performance of irrigated agriculture for farmers in the project area through improving on-farm water management, introducing modern irrigation technologies, and strengthening capacity of communities and farmers.
project_overview.total_financing: US$171 million (IDA Credit, SDR 123.4 million equivalent)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 150,000 farm families (around 1.1 million people) will benefit directly, mainly smallholder farmers with less than 5 ha of land (96% of KP farmers). Additional beneficiaries include rural communities, government institutions, and private sector companies.

primary_locations: All districts and tehsils of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) province
Newly merged Federally Administered Tribal Areas (FATA)
Chitral district (home to Kalash Indigenous Peoples)
Kohat to Dera Ismail Khan (South West Region)
Central KP and newly merged FATA areas
Batagram, Kohistan, Swat, Dir, Chitral and PATA areas (North Region)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: provincial/regional with focus on newly merged FATA areas
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - KP province has transitioned out of vulnerability and crises over past 8 years with substantial poverty reduction, but parts remain vulnerable. Newly merged FATA areas are among the poorest regions of Pakistan and are expected to retain tax-exempt status for 5 years. Security situation has improved considerably but region is still monitored closely by security apparatus.

key_components.component_name: Component A: Improvement of Community Irrigation Systems
key_components.description: Improvement of 14,000 watercourses through lining up to 50% of length using pre-cast parabolic segments (PCPS) technology. Includes 6,000 canal command watercourses, 1,400 large non-canal watercourses, 2,000 small non-canal watercourses, and 4,600 piped schemes. Farmers provide labor; government provides materials. Strengthens Water Users' Associations (WUAs).
key_components.financing: US$140.7 million (IDA: US$109.7 million, Farmer share: US$31.0 million)

key_components.component_name: Component B: Introduction of Modern Irrigation Technologies
key_components.description: Three subcomponents: B1) Installation of 10,000 acres of High-Efficiency Irrigation Systems (HEIS) such as drip and sprinkler systems (60% project, 40% farmer cost-share); B2) Construction of 5,000 on-farm water storage tanks/ponds for water conservation; B3) Provision of 500 precision land leveling (PLL) laser units to service providers on 50% cost-sharing basis.
key_components.financing: US$44.0 million (IDA: US$26.8 million, Farmer/Service provider share: US$17.2 million)

key_components.component_name: Component C: Capacity Building and Establishing Knowledge Base for Future Inclusive Growth
key_components.description: Two subcomponents: C1) Capacity building for farmers and government institutions in water resource management, crop production, value addition, ICT, high-value crop management, and value chain development; C2) Establishing knowledge base through Agribusiness Deep Dive assessment, feasibility study for Agribusiness Innovation Center, and studies on integrated water management, climate-smart agriculture, and gender.

key_components.component_name: Component D: Project Management and Monitoring
key_components.description: Three subcomponents: D1) Project management, operations, and administration including farmer mobilization, surveys, engineering designs, implementation supervision; D2) Project Implementation Supervision Consultancy (PISC) for third-party oversight and technical audits; D3) Independent Monitoring and Evaluation Consultancy for impact assessment and safeguard monitoring.

Supports preparedness and rapid response to disaster, emergency, and/or catastrophic events. Zero-cost component allowing rapid reallocation of credit proceeds from other components under streamlined procedures.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Department of Agriculture, Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (GoKP), specifically the Directorate General On-Farm Water Management (DGOFWM)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by Additional Chief Secretary (Planning and Development) with Secretaries of Agriculture, Irrigation, Local Government, Forestry, and Finance as members. Project Implementation Committee (PIC) chaired by DGOFWM. District Implementation Committees (DICs) in each district. District Rate Committees (DRCs) for material pricing. Three Regional Directors covering South West, Central, and North regions.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Community-Driven Development (CDD) for watercourse improvements and water storage tanks using output-based disbursement on unit rates. Framework Agreements (FA) for HEIS and PLL through open competitive procurement. World Bank Procurement Regulations apply. Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) system implemented. Procurement specialist to be hired within 3 months of effectiveness.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Independent M&E Consultants hired to monitor physical progress, conduct impact evaluations, and monitor environmental/social safeguards. Quarterly reports submitted within 45 days of quarter-end. Annual reports by September 30. Mid-term review by October 31, 2023. Implementation Completion Report within 6 months of closing. Baseline surveys, impact evaluation studies, and Project Management Information System (PMIS) to be established.

identified_risks.risk_description: Main risks relate to larger political and security situation in Pakistan. Although security situation has improved considerably, region is still monitored closely. Uncertainty about specific institutional arrangements and exact steps of FATA integration. Results of 2018 elections sustained reform-mindedness but some uncertainty remains.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong buy-in from political leadership for economic reforms. FATA merger expected to help remove regulatory differences and improve access to inputs/services over next 5 years. Project supports establishment and staffing of OFWM offices in newly merged districts.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risks stem from recent deterioration in macro-fiscal situation in Pakistan. Balance-of-payments position vulnerable at current reserve levels. PKR depreciation occurring. KP's fiscal situation vulnerable due to volatile non-tax revenues and increasing wage bill with unsustainable pension liabilities. New IMF program under discussion.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IMF program expected to support macroeconomic stability. Project design includes cost-sharing with farmers and private sector to reduce public burden. MFD approach to leverage private sector financing (estimated US$75 million).

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Risks arise from geographical spread of project, inclusion of new FATA territories without existing institutional setup, sheer number of contracts with communities, and high cost-sharing requirements for new activities (HEIS and PLL). OFWM Department needs capacity building in HEIS and PLL implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Department has sound experience with watercourse rehabilitation and can build on successful experience from Punjab PIPIP and Sindh SIAPEP projects. Government will ensure additional staff positions for FATA districts approved and included in non-development budget. Component D resources directed toward strengthening capacities. Project Implementation Supervision Consultants and M&E Consultants to provide oversight.

identified_risks.risk_description: Assessment concluded significant weaknesses in managing fiduciary aspects due to insufficient capacity of OFWM. Risks in financial management, accounting, reporting, and internal controls.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Nomination of qualified FM staff acceptable to World Bank from Department by negotiation, or hiring from Shared Services Unit (SSU) or market within 3 months of effectiveness. Timely submission of quarterly Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) and annual audited statements. Report-based disbursement with front-loading to Designated Account. Project on Government FMIS (SAP R3). Monthly reconciliations. Audits by Auditor General of Pakistan. Intensive FM supervision quarterly for first 2 years, then semi-annually.

identified_risks.risk_description: Main challenge is proper execution and management of thousands of schemes across province, work contracts, material purchases, installation, quality control, testing, certification, and payments.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Robust system of supervision, checking, and auditing at various levels. Output-based disbursement system to manage implementation issues. Adjustments to implementation arrangements based on lessons learned during initial implementation. Project Implementation Supervision Consultants for quality assurance and certification.

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risks related to sector policy implementation and coordination.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project aligned with National Water Policy (April 2018), KP Agriculture Policy and Strategy (2015-25), and Pakistan's Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDC). Strong government commitment to agriculture-led development.

identified_risks.risk_description: Project activities expected to cause some adverse environmental impacts during construction phase (noise, air, soil and water pollution, vegetation clearance, temporary water stoppages). During operation, potential impacts from water pollution, pesticide use, and excessive irrigation water application. Social impacts from minor land acquisition and temporary livelihood disruptions.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared identifying potential impacts and providing mitigation guidance. Integrated Pest Management Framework (IPMF) for pesticide management. Environmental and Social Impact Assessments (ESIAs) to be prepared during implementation. Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMPs) to be implemented. Environmental Specialist and Social Specialist in PIU core team. Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) for land acquisition. Indigenous Peoples Planning Framework (IPPF) for Chitral district. Grievance Redress Mechanism established.

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risks related to farmer participation, WUA functionality, and community buy-in for sustainability.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project uses Community-Driven Development approach with farmer participation in watercourse improvements. Water Users' Associations (WUAs) in driver's seat with 51% landholder membership requirement. Citizen Engagement mechanisms including real-time interaction platform, community participatory monitoring, and Grievance Redress Service. Project information campaign targeted at women to encourage WUA participation.

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-related Grievance Redress Service (GRS) established in project implementation system. Project activities at local and community level will inform stakeholders of their rights and channels to address concerns. Beneficiaries engaged in satisfaction surveys on GRS performance. GRS linked to provincial level system for referral where needed. World Bank's corporate GRS also available for complaints.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Comprehensive Gender Action Plan addressing three dimensions: (1) Farm productivity - women to receive training and technology upgrading support; 30% female trainers by year 2; 30% of capacity building beneficiaries to be women by project end (baseline 5%); (2) Decision-making on water resources - female staff to conduct information campaign encouraging women's participation in WUAs; target 50% of women landholders as WUA members and at least one woman on executive body of 30% of WUAs; (3) Data gaps - sector study to document women's role in agriculture and examine relationship between women's economic status improvement and gender-based violence. PIU to have 30% female staff with gender-sensitive workplace amenities (separate toilets, daycare, safe transport). Gender-specific indicators in results framework.
vulnerable_groups: Smallholder farmers (96% of KP farmers with less than 5 ha)
Women farmers (70% of farm work in KP conducted by women)
Poor and those just above poverty line
Newly merged FATA populations (among poorest regions of Pakistan)
Indigenous Peoples - Kalash in Chitral district

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed with conflict sensitivity considerations. KP province has transitioned out of vulnerability and crises over past 8 years with substantial poverty reduction (poverty fell from 73.8% in FY02 to 27% in FY14). However, parts of KP remain vulnerable to crises. Newly merged FATA areas are among poorest regions and expected to retain tax-exempt status for 5 years. Project explicitly aims to contribute to 'reduced vulnerability for groups at risk' (CPS Outcome 3.2) and social and economic inclusion. Project supports integration of newly merged FATA areas into KP institutional structure through establishment of OFWM offices and staffing. Resilience-building actions deliberately incorporated including climate-resilient irrigation infrastructure, institutional capacity building of WUAs, and climate-smart agriculture promotion.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Key fragility factors: (1) KP's integration of former FATA creates challenges and opportunities - FATA is one of poorest regions with expected tax-exempt status for 5 years, affecting KP's fiscal situation; (2) Uncertainty about specific institutional arrangements and exact steps of FATA integration; (3) Security apparatus still monitors region closely despite improved security situation; (4) High poverty rates in FATA areas; (5) Limited institutional capacity in newly merged areas; (6) Vulnerability to climate change and natural disasters (Pakistan ranks among top 10 most climate-vulnerable countries; 2010 floods caused US$300 million damage to KP agriculture). Project addresses fragility through: water security improvements, livelihood diversification, institutional capacity building, private sector engagement, and targeted support to vulnerable populations.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security situation in KP has improved considerably across the country and within province, but security apparatus still monitors region closely. Project design accounts for security context through: (1) Decentralized implementation with three Regional Directors and district-level offices to ensure presence in all areas; (2) Community-Driven Development approach building local institutional capacity and ownership; (3) Grievance Redress Mechanism for addressing community concerns; (4) Citizen Engagement mechanisms for two-way interaction between citizens and state; (5) Support for integration of newly merged FATA areas into KP system to improve governance and service delivery.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Minor displacement risks identified. Small parcels of land may be acquired for water storage and/or farm products storage. In most cases, land needs will be met through community or voluntary land donations (VLD). Certain interventions (e.g., rehabilitation of existing watercourses) may involve minor temporary impacts on livelihoods due to restricted access or temporary dislocation requiring small-scale compensation. However, impacts not expected to be large-scale. Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared, consulted upon, and disclosed to address any involuntary resettlement issues. No refugee populations mentioned as project beneficiaries, though project area includes newly merged FATA which has experienced conflict-related displacement historically.
